* {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉}}(Nagano Prefecture)
* Ebino Kōgen Onsen (Miyazaki Prefecture)
== Accidents == Hydrogen sulfide springs can release hydrogen sulfide gas into the air, which may cause {{ill|poisoning|en|Poisoning}}<nowiki/>when inhaled in large concentrations or for extended periods. Proper {{ill|ventilation|en|Ventilation (architecture)}} is essential in indoor baths. In outdoor springs or {{ill|wilderness hot springs|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs}}, high-concentration hydrogen sulfide gas, which is heavier than air, tends to accumulate in depressions or hollows, potentially leading to fatal poisoning incidents.<ref group="注">Between 1935 and 1985, nine deaths occurred at {{ill|Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|ja|地獄谷 (立山町)}} .</ref> Snow-covered onsen in winter can also trap gas, increasing the risk of poisoning.<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html Hydrogen Sulfide Poisoning Incidents]</ref> {{Seealso|Hydrogen sulfide#Toxicity}}

{{afc comment|1=This is questionable as I think it requires [[WP:MEDRS]] and I am surprised that jawiki has these claims not only not medically sourced but without medically reliable sources[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:10, 14 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|An example of a sulfur spring: the "Tenkei no Yu" at {{ill|Takayu Onsen|ja|高湯温泉}} ]] '''Sulfur Spring''' (硫黄泉, Iōsen) is a classification of {{ill|onsen|ja|温泉}}<nowiki/>based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質}} labeling system. It is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉}} due to its unique components. == Overview == Sulfur springs are characterized by a rotten egg smell caused by {{ill|hydrogen sulfide|en|Hydrogen sulfide}}<nowiki/>and the frequent occurrence of cloudy water due to {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana}} (mineral deposits). While the labeling system categorizes all sulfur springs under a single term, they are generally divided into two main types based on the presence or absence of hydrogen sulfide: sulfur-based springs and '''hydrogen sulfide springs.''' Identification of the type can be made using the new or old water quality labels.
The pH range of sulfur springs varies widely. Springs with higher acidity (lower pH) are typically hydrogen sulfide springs, while those with higher alkalinity are often sulfur-based. Notable high-sulfur concentration onsen in Japan include {{ill|Takayu Onsen|ja|高湯温泉}}(Fukushima), {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉}} (Gunma), and {{ill|Tsukioka Onsen|ja|月岡温泉}} (Niigata). == Definition == Sulfur springs are defined as containing at least 2 mg of total sulfur (S) per kilogram of water, including HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, and H<sub>2</sub>S.<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ Definition and Protection of Onsen - Ministry of the Environment]</ref>
== Comparison of Old and New Labels == {| class="wikitable" |+ Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! Old Label !! New Label !! Abbreviated Label |- | Sulfur Spring || Sulfur Spring || S Spring |- | Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple S Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub> Spring |- | Hydrogen Sulfide Spring || Sulfur Spring (Hydrogen Sulfide Type) || S Spring (H<sub>2</sub>S Type) |}
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
Benefits associated with sulfur springs include the following:
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General indications:''' Chronic {{ill|skin diseases|en|Skin disease}}, chronic {{ill|gynecological disorders|en|Gynecological disorder}} , {{ill|cuts|en|Cut (wound)}} , and {{ill|diabetes|en|Diabetes mellitus}} .
* '''Hydrogen sulfide type:''' Effective for {{ill|hypertension|en|Hypertension}}<nowiki/>and {{ill|arteriosclerosis|en|Arteriosclerosis}} .
==== Drinking ==== * Beneficial for diabetes, {{ill|gout|en|Gout}}, {{ill|constipation|en|Constipation}} , and {{ill|diarrhea|en|Diarrhea}} . === Contraindications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General contraindications:''' Overly sensitive {{ill|skin|en|Human skin}}<nowiki/>or {{ill|mucous membranes|en|Mucous membrane}}, particularly in individuals with {{ill|photosensitivity|en|Photosensitivity}} .<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/pdf/pamph_full_en.pdf Onsen_ABC]</ref> == Representative Onsen == * {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|ja|川湯温泉 (北海道)}}
* {{ill|Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja|箱根湯本温泉}}(Kanagawa Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|ja|日光湯元温泉}}(Tochigi Prefecture)
* {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|ja|草津温泉}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉}}(Nagano Prefecture)
* Ebino Kōgen Onsen (Miyazaki Prefecture)
== Accidents == Hydrogen sulfide springs can release hydrogen sulfide gas into the air, which may cause {{ill|poisoning|en|Poisoning}}<nowiki/>when inhaled in large concentrations or for extended periods. Proper {{ill|ventilation|en|Ventilation (architecture)}} is essential in indoor baths. In outdoor springs or {{ill|wilderness hot springs|ja|野湯|1=Wilderness hot springs}}, high-concentration hydrogen sulfide gas, which is heavier than air, tends to accumulate in depressions or hollows, potentially leading to fatal poisoning incidents.<ref group="注">Between 1935 and 1985, nine deaths occurred at {{ill|Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|ja|地獄谷 (立山町)}} .</ref> Snow-covered onsen in winter can also trap gas, increasing the risk of poisoning.<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html Hydrogen Sulfide Poisoning Incidents]</ref> {{Seealso|Hydrogen sulfide#Toxicity}}
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{reflist|group="注"}}
=== References === {{reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Sessho-seki|ja|殺生石}}

revision.text:
[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|硫黄泉の例、[[高湯温泉]]・玉子湯の「天渓の湯」]] '''硫黄泉'''（いおうせん）は、掲示用泉質名に基づく[[温泉]]の[[泉質]]の分類の一種。特殊成分を含む[[温泉#療養泉|療養泉]]に分類される。
== 概要 == [[卵]]が腐ったような臭い、また湧出後湯船にて[[湯の花]]により白濁する温泉も多いのが特徴。掲示用泉質名では硫黄泉と一括りにされるが、[[硫化水素]]の含有の有無により、全く含まない硫黄泉と、これを含む'''硫化水素泉'''に大別される。硫黄型および硫化水素型の判別は新泉質名、または旧泉質名を確認することで判別できる。[[水素イオン指数|pH]]の分布は広いが、[[酸|酸度]]が高いものは硫化水素型に多く、[[塩基|アルカリ性]]を示すものは硫黄型に多い。
日本国内で特に[[硫黄]][[濃度]]の高い温泉地として[[高湯温泉]]（[[福島県]][[福島市]]）、[[万座温泉]]（[[群馬県]][[嬬恋村]]）、[[月岡温泉]]（[[新潟県]][[新発田市]]）の3か所が挙げられる。
== 泉質の定義 == 温泉1kg中に総硫黄 (S) [HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, H<sub>2</sub>S に対応するもの] を2mg以上含有<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ 温泉の保護と利用 - 温泉の定義][[環境省]]自然環境局自然環境整備担当参事官室</ref>。
== 新旧泉質名 == 新旧泉質名では、以下に分類される。
{| class="wikitable" |+ 新旧泉質名の対応<ref>{{Cite web|和書| |title=参考資料２新旧泉質名対照 |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=環境省自然環境局自然環境整備課温泉地保護利用推進室 |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" id="旧泉質名" | 旧泉質名 ! scope="col" id="新泉質名" | 新泉質名 ! scope="col" id="略記泉質名" | 略記泉質名 |- style="background-color:#eef" | 硫黄泉 || 硫黄泉 || S泉 |- | 単純硫黄泉 || 単純硫黄泉 || 単純S泉 |- | 含食塩－硫黄泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－塩化物泉 || 含S-Na-Cl泉 |- | 含食塩重曹－硫黄泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－塩化物・炭酸水素塩泉 || 含S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub>泉 |- style="background-color:#eef" |- | 硫化水素泉 || 硫黄泉（硫化水素型）|| S泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 単純硫化水素泉 || 単純硫黄泉（硫化水素型）|| 単純S泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 酸性硫化水素泉 || 酸性－含硫黄・（ナトリウム）－硫酸塩泉（硫 酸性-化水素型）||含S(Na)-SO<sub>4</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 土類硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム（・マグネシウム）－炭酸水素塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca(･Mg)-HCO<sub>3</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含食塩－土類硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム（・マグネシウム）・ナトリウム－炭酸水素塩・塩化物泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca(･Mg)･Na-HCO<sub>3</sub>･Cl泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含石膏－土類硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム（・マグネシウム）－炭酸水素塩・硫酸塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca(･Mg)-HCO<sub>3</sub>･SO<sub>4</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 重曹硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－炭酸水素塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na-HCO<sub>3</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含芒硝－重曹硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－炭酸水素塩・硫酸塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na-HCO<sub>3</sub>･SO<sub>4</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 塩化物硫化水素泉 || || |- | 食塩硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－塩化物泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na-Cl泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 塩化土類硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム（・マグネシウム）－塩化物泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca(･Mg)-Cl泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含重曹－食塩硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－塩化物・炭酸水素塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含石膏－食塩硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム・カルシウム－塩化物・硫酸塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na･Ca-Cl･SO<sub>4</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 硫酸塩硫化水素泉 || || |- | 石膏硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム硫酸塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca-SO<sub>4</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含食塩－芒硝硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－硫酸塩・塩化物泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na-SO<sub>4</sub>･Cl泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含食塩－石膏硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム・ナトリウム－硫酸塩・塩化物泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca･Na-SO<sub>4</sub>･Cl泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |}
== 効能 == ''※[[効能]]はその効果を万人に保証するものではない''
泉質に基づく効能として、以下が挙げられる。
=== 適応症 === ==== 浴用 ==== * [[一般的適応症]]のほか、慢性[[皮膚病]]、慢性[[婦人病]]、[[切り傷]]、[[糖尿病]]。 * 硫化水素型では他に、[[高血圧|高血圧症]]、[[動脈硬化症]]。
==== 飲用 ==== * 糖尿病、[[痛風]]、[[便秘]]、[[下痢]]。
=== 禁忌症 === ==== 浴用 ==== * [[一般的適応症#一般的禁忌症|一般的禁忌症]]のほか、硫化水素型では[[皮膚]]・[[粘膜]]の過敏な人、特に[[光線過敏|光線過敏症]]の人。
== 代表的な温泉地 == * [[川湯温泉 (北海道)|川湯温泉]]（北海道） * [[箱根湯本温泉]]（神奈川県） * [[日光湯元温泉]]（栃木県） * [[草津温泉]]（群馬県） * [[万座温泉]]（群馬県） * [[野沢温泉]]（長野県） * えびの高原温泉（宮崎県）
== 事故 == 硫化水素泉の場合、空気中に放出される硫化水素ガスを長時間、または高濃度のものを吸引すると[[中毒]]を起こす。そのため、内風呂（屋内の浴場）では十分に[[換気]]が行われなければならない。また[[野湯]]の場合、[[空気]]より[[比重]]の大きい高濃度の硫化水素ガス（比重1.1905）は窪みや穴状の[[地形]]の中にあるとが溜りやすく、過去には中毒死亡事故も起きている<ref group="注">[[地獄谷 (立山町)|立山地獄谷温泉]]では、[[1935年]]（昭和10年）から[[1985年]]（昭和60年）までの50年の間に9人が死亡している。</ref>。さらに冬季には地形にかかわらず、[[積雪]]で温泉の周りが囲まれた状態となっても同様のことが起きる恐れがある<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html 主な硫化水素事故]</ref>。 {{Seealso|硫化水素#毒性}}
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{reflist|group="注"}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[殺生石]]
{{温泉}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:いおうせん}} [[Category:温泉の泉質]] [[Category:硫黄]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[硫黄泉]] to [[Sulfur spring]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|硫黄泉の例、[[高湯温泉]]・玉子湯の「天渓の湯」]] '''硫黄泉'''（いおうせん）は、掲示用泉質名に基づく[[温泉]]の[[泉質]]の分類の一種。特殊成分を含む[[温泉#療養泉|療養泉]]に分類される。
== 概要 == [[卵]]が腐ったような臭い、また湧出後湯船にて[[湯の花]]により白濁する温泉も多いのが特徴。掲示用泉質名では硫黄泉と一括りにされるが、[[硫化水素]]の含有の有無により、全く含まない硫黄泉と、これを含む'''硫化水素泉'''に大別される。硫黄型および硫化水素型の判別は新泉質名、または旧泉質名を確認することで判別できる。[[水素イオン指数|pH]]の分布は広いが、[[酸|酸度]]が高いものは硫化水素型に多く、[[塩基|アルカリ性]]を示すものは硫黄型に多い。
日本国内で特に[[硫黄]][[濃度]]の高い温泉地として[[高湯温泉]]（[[福島県]][[福島市]]）、[[万座温泉]]（[[群馬県]][[嬬恋村]]）、[[月岡温泉]]（[[新潟県]][[新発田市]]）の3か所が挙げられる。
== 泉質の定義 == 温泉1kg中に総硫黄 (S) [HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, H<sub>2</sub>S に対応するもの] を2mg以上含有<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ 温泉の保護と利用 - 温泉の定義][[環境省]]自然環境局自然環境整備担当参事官室</ref>。
== 新旧泉質名 == 新旧泉質名では、以下に分類される。
{| class="wikitable" |+ 新旧泉質名の対応<ref>{{Cite web|和書| |title=参考資料２新旧泉質名対照 |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=環境省自然環境局自然環境整備課温泉地保護利用推進室 |accessdate=2018-03-26 }}</ref> ! scope="col" id="旧泉質名" | 旧泉質名 ! scope="col" id="新泉質名" | 新泉質名 ! scope="col" id="略記泉質名" | 略記泉質名 |- style="background-color:#eef" | 硫黄泉 || 硫黄泉 || S泉 |- | 単純硫黄泉 || 単純硫黄泉 || 単純S泉 |- | 含食塩－硫黄泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－塩化物泉 || 含S-Na-Cl泉 |- | 含食塩重曹－硫黄泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－塩化物・炭酸水素塩泉 || 含S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub>泉 |- style="background-color:#eef" |- | 硫化水素泉 || 硫黄泉（硫化水素型）|| S泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 単純硫化水素泉 || 単純硫黄泉（硫化水素型）|| 単純S泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 酸性硫化水素泉 || 酸性－含硫黄・（ナトリウム）－硫酸塩泉（硫 酸性-化水素型）||含S(Na)-SO<sub>4</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 土類硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム（・マグネシウム）－炭酸水素塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca(･Mg)-HCO<sub>3</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含食塩－土類硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム（・マグネシウム）・ナトリウム－炭酸水素塩・塩化物泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca(･Mg)･Na-HCO<sub>3</sub>･Cl泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含石膏－土類硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム（・マグネシウム）－炭酸水素塩・硫酸塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca(･Mg)-HCO<sub>3</sub>･SO<sub>4</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 重曹硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－炭酸水素塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na-HCO<sub>3</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含芒硝－重曹硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－炭酸水素塩・硫酸塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na-HCO<sub>3</sub>･SO<sub>4</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 塩化物硫化水素泉 || || |- | 食塩硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－塩化物泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na-Cl泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 塩化土類硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム（・マグネシウム）－塩化物泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca(･Mg)-Cl泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含重曹－食塩硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－塩化物・炭酸水素塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含石膏－食塩硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム・カルシウム－塩化物・硫酸塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na･Ca-Cl･SO<sub>4</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 硫酸塩硫化水素泉 || || |- | 石膏硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム硫酸塩泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca-SO<sub>4</sub>泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含食塩－芒硝硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－ナトリウム－硫酸塩・塩化物泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Na-SO<sub>4</sub>･Cl泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |- | 含食塩－石膏硫化水素泉 || 含硫黄－カルシウム・ナトリウム－硫酸塩・塩化物泉（硫化水素型）|| 含S-Ca･Na-SO<sub>4</sub>･Cl泉(H<sub>2</sub>S型) |}
== 効能 == ''※[[効能]]はその効果を万人に保証するものではない''
泉質に基づく効能として、以下が挙げられる。
=== 適応症 === ==== 浴用 ==== * [[一般的適応症]]のほか、慢性[[皮膚病]]、慢性[[婦人病]]、[[切り傷]]、[[糖尿病]]。 * 硫化水素型では他に、[[高血圧|高血圧症]]、[[動脈硬化症]]。
==== 飲用 ==== * 糖尿病、[[痛風]]、[[便秘]]、[[下痢]]。
=== 禁忌症 === ==== 浴用 ==== * [[一般的適応症#一般的禁忌症|一般的禁忌症]]のほか、硫化水素型では[[皮膚]]・[[粘膜]]の過敏な人、特に[[光線過敏|光線過敏症]]の人。
== 代表的な温泉地 == * [[川湯温泉 (北海道)|川湯温泉]]（北海道） * [[箱根湯本温泉]]（神奈川県） * [[日光湯元温泉]]（栃木県） * [[草津温泉]]（群馬県） * [[万座温泉]]（群馬県） * [[野沢温泉]]（長野県） * えびの高原温泉（宮崎県）
== 事故 == 硫化水素泉の場合、空気中に放出される硫化水素ガスを長時間、または高濃度のものを吸引すると[[中毒]]を起こす。そのため、内風呂（屋内の浴場）では十分に[[換気]]が行われなければならない。また[[野湯]]の場合、[[空気]]より[[比重]]の大きい高濃度の硫化水素ガス（比重1.1905）は窪みや穴状の[[地形]]の中にあるとが溜りやすく、過去には中毒死亡事故も起きている<ref group="注">[[地獄谷 (立山町)|立山地獄谷温泉]]では、[[1935年]]（昭和10年）から[[1985年]]（昭和60年）までの50年の間に9人が死亡している。</ref>。さらに冬季には地形にかかわらず、[[積雪]]で温泉の周りが囲まれた状態となっても同様のことが起きる恐れがある<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html 主な硫化水素事故]</ref>。 {{Seealso|硫化水素#毒性}}
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{reflist|group="注"}} === 出典 === {{reflist}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:硫黄泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11587679]] Wikidata name: Sulfur Spring [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This is questionable as I think it requires [[WP:MEDRS]] and I am surprised that jawiki has these claims not only not medically sourced but without medically reliable sources[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:10, 14 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|An example of a sulfur spring: the "Tenkei no Yu" at [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]]'''Sulfur Spring''' (硫黄泉, Iōsen) is a classification of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]<nowiki/>based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} labeling system. It is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medical spring|qq=|1=onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q655311}} due to its unique components. == Overview == Sulfur springs are characterized by a rotten egg smell caused by {{ill|hydrogen sulfide|en|Hydrogen sulfide|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hydrogen sulfide|qq=}}<nowiki/>and the frequent occurrence of cloudy water due to {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}} (mineral deposits). While the labeling system categorizes all sulfur springs under a single term, they are generally divided into two main types based on the presence or absence of hydrogen sulfide: sulfur-based springs and '''hydrogen sulfide springs.''' Identification of the type can be made using the new or old water quality labels.
The pH range of sulfur springs varies widely. Springs with higher acidity (lower pH) are typically hydrogen sulfide springs, while those with higher alkalinity are often sulfur-based. Notable high-sulfur concentration onsen in Japan include [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]](Fukushima), {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}} (Gunma), and {{ill|Tsukioka Onsen|ja|月岡温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukioka Onsen|qq=}} (Niigata). == Definition == Sulfur springs are defined as containing at least 2 mg of total sulfur (S) per kilogram of water, including HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, and H<sub>2</sub>S.<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ Definition and Protection of Onsen - Ministry of the Environment]</ref>
== Comparison of Old and New Labels == {| class="wikitable" |+ Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! Old Label !! New Label !! Abbreviated Label |- | Sulfur Spring || Sulfur Spring || S Spring |- | Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple S Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub> Spring |- | Hydrogen Sulfide Spring || Sulfur Spring (Hydrogen Sulfide Type) || S Spring (H<sub>2</sub>S Type) |}
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
Benefits associated with sulfur springs include the following:
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General indications:''' Chronic {{ill|skin diseases|en|Skin disease|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin diseases|qq=}}, chronic {{ill|gynecological disorders|en|Gynecological disorder|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gynecological disorders|qq=}} , {{ill|cuts|en|Cut (wound)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cuts|qq=}} , and {{ill|diabetes|en|Diabetes mellitus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diabetes|qq=}} .
* '''Hydrogen sulfide type:''' Effective for {{ill|hypertension|en|Hypertension|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hypertension|qq=}}<nowiki/>and {{ill|arteriosclerosis|en|Arteriosclerosis|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/arteriosclerosis|qq=}}
==== Drinking ==== * Beneficial for diabetes, {{ill|gout|en|Gout|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gout|qq=}}, {{ill|constipation|en|Constipation|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/constipation|qq=}} , and {{ill|diarrhea|en|Diarrhea|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diarrhea|qq=}} . === Contraindications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General contraindications:''' Overly sensitive {{ill|skin|en|Human skin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin|qq=}}<nowiki/>or {{ill|mucous membranes|en|Mucous membrane|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mucous membranes|qq=}}, particularly in individuals with {{ill|photosensitivity|en|Photosensitivity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/photosensitivity|qq=}} .<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/pdf/pamph_full_en.pdf Onsen_ABC]</ref> == Representative Onsen == * {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|ja|川湯温泉 (北海道)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|qq=}}
* {{ill|Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja|箱根湯本温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Yumoto Onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱根温泉|1=Hakone Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908993}}(Kanagawa Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|ja|日光湯元温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|qq=}}(Tochigi Prefecture)
* [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]](Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}}(Nagano Prefecture)
* Ebino Kōgen Onsen (Miyazaki Prefecture)
== Accidents == Hydrogen sulfide springs can release hydrogen sulfide gas into the air, which may cause {{ill|poisoning|en|Poisoning|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/poisoning|qq=}}<nowiki/>when inhaled in large concentrations or for extended periods. Proper {{ill|ventilation|en|Ventilation (architecture)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ventilation|qq=}} is essential in indoor baths. In outdoor springs or {{ill|wilderness hot springs|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wilderness hot springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, high-concentration hydrogen sulfide gas, which is heavier than air, tends to accumulate in depressions or hollows, potentially leading to fatal poisoning incidents.<ref group="注">Between 1935 and 1985, nine deaths occurred at {{ill|Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|ja|地獄谷 (立山町)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|qq=}} .</ref> Snow-covered onsen in winter can also trap gas, increasing the risk of poisoning.<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html Hydrogen Sulfide Poisoning Incidents]</ref> {{Seealso|Hydrogen sulfide#Toxicity}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Sessho-seki|Sessho-seki]]
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{reflist|group="注"}}
=== References === {{reflist}} *

[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|An example of a sulfur spring: the "Tenkei no Yu" at [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]]'''Sulfur Spring''' (硫黄泉, Iōsen) is a classification of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]<nowiki/>based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} labeling system. It is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medical spring|qq=|1=onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q655311}} due to its unique components. == Overview == Sulfur springs are characterized by a rotten egg smell caused by {{ill|hydrogen sulfide|en|Hydrogen sulfide|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hydrogen sulfide|qq=}}<nowiki/>and the frequent occurrence of cloudy water due to {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}} (mineral deposits). While the labeling system categorizes all sulfur springs under a single term, they are generally divided into two main types based on the presence or absence of hydrogen sulfide: sulfur-based springs and '''hydrogen sulfide springs.''' Identification of the type can be made using the new or old water quality labels.
The pH range of sulfur springs varies widely. Springs with higher acidity (lower pH) are typically hydrogen sulfide springs, while those with higher alkalinity are often sulfur-based. Notable high-sulfur concentration onsen in Japan include [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]](Fukushima), {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}} (Gunma), and {{ill|Tsukioka Onsen|ja|月岡温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukioka Onsen|qq=}} (Niigata). == Definition == Sulfur springs are defined as containing at least 2 mg of total sulfur (S) per kilogram of water, including HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, and H<sub>2</sub>S.<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ Definition and Protection of Onsen - Ministry of the Environment]</ref>
== Comparison of Old and New Labels == {| class="wikitable" |+ Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! Old Label !! New Label !! Abbreviated Label |- | Sulfur Spring || Sulfur Spring || S Spring |- | Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple S Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub> Spring |- | Hydrogen Sulfide Spring || Sulfur Spring (Hydrogen Sulfide Type) || S Spring (H<sub>2</sub>S Type) |}
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
Benefits associated with sulfur springs include the following:
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General indications:''' Chronic {{ill|skin diseases|en|Skin disease|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin diseases|qq=}}, chronic {{ill|gynecological disorders|en|Gynecological disorder|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gynecological disorders|qq=}} , {{ill|cuts|en|Cut (wound)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cuts|qq=}} , and {{ill|diabetes|en|Diabetes mellitus|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diabetes|qq=}} .
* '''Hydrogen sulfide type:''' Effective for {{ill|hypertension|en|Hypertension|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hypertension|qq=}}<nowiki/>and {{ill|arteriosclerosis|en|Arteriosclerosis|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/arteriosclerosis|qq=}}
==== Drinking ==== * Beneficial for diabetes, {{ill|gout|en|Gout|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gout|qq=}}, {{ill|constipation|en|Constipation|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/constipation|qq=}} , and {{ill|diarrhea|en|Diarrhea|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diarrhea|qq=}} . === Contraindications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General contraindications:''' Overly sensitive {{ill|skin|en|Human skin|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin|qq=}}<nowiki/>or {{ill|mucous membranes|en|Mucous membrane|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mucous membranes|qq=}}, particularly in individuals with {{ill|photosensitivity|en|Photosensitivity|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/photosensitivity|qq=}} .<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/pdf/pamph_full_en.pdf Onsen_ABC]</ref> == Representative Onsen == * {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|ja|川湯温泉 (北海道)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|qq=}}
* {{ill|Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja|箱根湯本温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Yumoto Onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱根温泉|1=Hakone Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908993}}(Kanagawa Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|ja|日光湯元温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|qq=}}(Tochigi Prefecture)
* [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]](Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}}(Nagano Prefecture)
* Ebino Kōgen Onsen (Miyazaki Prefecture)
== Accidents == Hydrogen sulfide springs can release hydrogen sulfide gas into the air, which may cause {{ill|poisoning|en|Poisoning|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/poisoning|qq=}}<nowiki/>when inhaled in large concentrations or for extended periods. Proper {{ill|ventilation|en|Ventilation (architecture)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ventilation|qq=}} is essential in indoor baths. In outdoor springs or {{ill|wilderness hot springs|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wilderness hot springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, high-concentration hydrogen sulfide gas, which is heavier than air, tends to accumulate in depressions or hollows, potentially leading to fatal poisoning incidents.<ref group="注">Between 1935 and 1985, nine deaths occurred at {{ill|Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|ja|地獄谷 (立山町)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|qq=}} .</ref> Snow-covered onsen in winter can also trap gas, increasing the risk of poisoning.<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html Hydrogen Sulfide Poisoning Incidents]</ref> {{Seealso|Hydrogen sulfide#Toxicity}}

[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|An example of a sulfur spring: the "Tenkei no Yu" at [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]]'''Sulfur Spring''' (硫黄泉, Iōsen) is a classification of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]<nowiki/>based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} labeling system. It is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medical spring|1=onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q655311}} due to its unique components. == Overview == Sulfur springs are characterized by a rotten egg smell caused by {{ill|hydrogen sulfide|en|Hydrogen sulfide|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hydrogen sulfide|qq=}}<nowiki/>and the frequent occurrence of cloudy water due to {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}} (mineral deposits). While the labeling system categorizes all sulfur springs under a single term, they are generally divided into two main types based on the presence or absence of hydrogen sulfide: sulfur-based springs and '''hydrogen sulfide springs.''' Identification of the type can be made using the new or old water quality labels.
The pH range of sulfur springs varies widely. Springs with higher acidity (lower pH) are typically hydrogen sulfide springs, while those with higher alkalinity are often sulfur-based. Notable high-sulfur concentration onsen in Japan include [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]](Fukushima), {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}} (Gunma), and {{ill|Tsukioka Onsen|ja|月岡温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukioka Onsen|qq=}} (Niigata). == Definition == Sulfur springs are defined as containing at least 2 mg of total sulfur (S) per kilogram of water, including HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, and H<sub>2</sub>S.<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ Definition and Protection of Onsen - Ministry of the Environment]</ref>

[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|An example of a sulfur spring: the "Tenkei no Yu" at [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]]'''Sulfur Spring''' (硫黄泉, Iōsen) is a classification of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]<nowiki/>based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} labeling system. It is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medical spring|1=onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q655311}} due to its unique components. == Overview == Sulfur springs are characterized by a rotten egg smell caused by {{ill|hydrogen sulfide|en|Hydrogen sulfide|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hydrogen sulfide|qq=}}<nowiki/>and the frequent occurrence of cloudy water due to {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}} (mineral deposits). While the labeling system categorizes all sulfur springs under a single term, they are generally divided into two main types based on the presence or absence of hydrogen sulfide: sulfur-based springs and '''hydrogen sulfide springs.''' Identification of the type can be made using the new or old water quality labels.
The pH range of sulfur springs varies widely. Springs with higher acidity (lower pH) are typically hydrogen sulfide springs, while those with higher alkalinity are often sulfur-based. Notable high-sulfur concentration onsen in Japan include [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]](Fukushima), {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}} (Gunma), and {{ill|Tsukioka Onsen|ja|月岡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukioka Onsen|qq=}} (Niigata). == Definition == Sulfur springs are defined as containing at least 2 mg of total sulfur (S) per kilogram of water, including HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, and H<sub>2</sub>S.<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ Definition and Protection of Onsen - Ministry of the Environment]</ref>
== Comparison of Old and New Labels == {| class="wikitable" |+ Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! Old Label !! New Label !! Abbreviated Label |- | Sulfur Spring || Sulfur Spring || S Spring |- | Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple S Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub> Spring |- | Hydrogen Sulfide Spring || Sulfur Spring (Hydrogen Sulfide Type) || S Spring (H<sub>2</sub>S Type) |}
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
Benefits associated with sulfur springs include the following:
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General indications:''' Chronic {{ill|skin diseases|en|Skin disease|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin diseases|qq=}}, chronic {{ill|gynecological disorders|en|Gynecological disorder|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gynecological disorders|qq=}} , {{ill|cuts|en|Cut (wound)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cuts|qq=}} , and {{ill|diabetes|en|Diabetes mellitus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diabetes|qq=}} .
* '''Hydrogen sulfide type:''' Effective for {{ill|hypertension|en|Hypertension|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hypertension|qq=}}<nowiki/>and {{ill|arteriosclerosis|en|Arteriosclerosis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/arteriosclerosis|qq=}}
==== Drinking ==== * Beneficial for diabetes, {{ill|gout|en|Gout|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gout|qq=}}, {{ill|constipation|en|Constipation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/constipation|qq=}} , and {{ill|diarrhea|en|Diarrhea|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diarrhea|qq=}} . === Contraindications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General contraindications:''' Overly sensitive {{ill|skin|en|Human skin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin|qq=}}<nowiki/>or {{ill|mucous membranes|en|Mucous membrane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mucous membranes|qq=}}, particularly in individuals with {{ill|photosensitivity|en|Photosensitivity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/photosensitivity|qq=}} .<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/pdf/pamph_full_en.pdf Onsen_ABC]</ref> == Representative Onsen == * {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|ja|川湯温泉 (北海道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|qq=}}
* {{ill|Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja|箱根湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱根温泉|1=Hakone Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908993}}(Kanagawa Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|ja|日光湯元温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|qq=}}(Tochigi Prefecture)
* [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]](Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}}(Nagano Prefecture)
* Ebino Kōgen Onsen (Miyazaki Prefecture)
== Accidents == Hydrogen sulfide springs can release hydrogen sulfide gas into the air, which may cause {{ill|poisoning|en|Poisoning|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/poisoning|qq=}}<nowiki/>when inhaled in large concentrations or for extended periods. Proper {{ill|ventilation|en|Ventilation (architecture)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ventilation|qq=}} is essential in indoor baths. In outdoor springs or {{ill|wilderness hot springs|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wilderness hot springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, high-concentration hydrogen sulfide gas, which is heavier than air, tends to accumulate in depressions or hollows, potentially leading to fatal poisoning incidents.<ref group="注">Between 1935 and 1985, nine deaths occurred at {{ill|Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|ja|地獄谷 (立山町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|qq=}} .</ref> Snow-covered onsen in winter can also trap gas, increasing the risk of poisoning.<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html Hydrogen Sulfide Poisoning Incidents]</ref> {{Seealso|Hydrogen sulfide#Toxicity}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Sessho-seki|Sessho-seki]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:硫黄泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11587679]] Wikidata name: Sulfur Spring [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This is questionable as I think it requires [[WP:MEDRS]] and I am surprised that jawiki has these claims not only not medically sourced but without medically reliable sources[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:10, 14 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|An example of a sulfur spring: the "Tenkei no Yu" at [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]]'''Sulfur Spring''' (硫黄泉, Iōsen) is a classification of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]<nowiki/>based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} labeling system. It is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medical spring|1=onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q655311}} due to its unique components. == Overview == Sulfur springs are characterized by a rotten egg smell caused by {{ill|hydrogen sulfide|en|Hydrogen sulfide|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hydrogen sulfide|qq=}}<nowiki/>and the frequent occurrence of cloudy water due to {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} (mineral deposits). While the labeling system categorizes all sulfur springs under a single term, they are generally divided into two main types based on the presence or absence of hydrogen sulfide: sulfur-based springs and '''hydrogen sulfide springs.''' Identification of the type can be made using the new or old water quality labels.
The pH range of sulfur springs varies widely. Springs with higher acidity (lower pH) are typically hydrogen sulfide springs, while those with higher alkalinity are often sulfur-based. Notable high-sulfur concentration onsen in Japan include [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]](Fukushima), {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}} (Gunma), and {{ill|Tsukioka Onsen|ja|月岡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukioka Onsen|qq=}} (Niigata). == Definition == Sulfur springs are defined as containing at least 2 mg of total sulfur (S) per kilogram of water, including HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, and H<sub>2</sub>S.<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ Definition and Protection of Onsen - Ministry of the Environment]</ref>
== Comparison of Old and New Labels == {| class="wikitable" |+ Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! Old Label !! New Label !! Abbreviated Label |- | Sulfur Spring || Sulfur Spring || S Spring |- | Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple S Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub> Spring |- | Hydrogen Sulfide Spring || Sulfur Spring (Hydrogen Sulfide Type) || S Spring (H<sub>2</sub>S Type) |}
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
Benefits associated with sulfur springs include the following:
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General indications:''' Chronic {{ill|skin diseases|en|Skin disease|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin diseases|qq=}}, chronic {{ill|gynecological disorders|en|Gynecological disorder|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gynecological disorders|qq=}} , {{ill|cuts|en|Cut (wound)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cuts|qq=}} , and {{ill|diabetes|en|Diabetes mellitus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diabetes|qq=}} .
* '''Hydrogen sulfide type:''' Effective for {{ill|hypertension|en|Hypertension|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hypertension|qq=}}<nowiki/>and {{ill|arteriosclerosis|en|Arteriosclerosis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/arteriosclerosis|qq=}}
==== Drinking ==== * Beneficial for diabetes, {{ill|gout|en|Gout|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gout|qq=}}, {{ill|constipation|en|Constipation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/constipation|qq=}} , and {{ill|diarrhea|en|Diarrhea|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diarrhea|qq=}} . === Contraindications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General contraindications:''' Overly sensitive {{ill|skin|en|Human skin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin|qq=}}<nowiki/>or {{ill|mucous membranes|en|Mucous membrane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mucous membranes|qq=}}, particularly in individuals with {{ill|photosensitivity|en|Photosensitivity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/photosensitivity|qq=}} .<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/pdf/pamph_full_en.pdf Onsen_ABC]</ref> == Representative Onsen == * {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|ja|川湯温泉 (北海道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|qq=}}
* {{ill|Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja|箱根湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱根温泉|1=Hakone Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908993}}(Kanagawa Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|ja|日光湯元温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|qq=}}(Tochigi Prefecture)
* [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]](Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}}(Nagano Prefecture)
* Ebino Kōgen Onsen (Miyazaki Prefecture)
== Accidents == Hydrogen sulfide springs can release hydrogen sulfide gas into the air, which may cause {{ill|poisoning|en|Poisoning|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/poisoning|qq=}}<nowiki/>when inhaled in large concentrations or for extended periods. Proper {{ill|ventilation|en|Ventilation (architecture)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ventilation|qq=}} is essential in indoor baths. In outdoor springs or {{ill|wilderness hot springs|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wilderness hot springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, high-concentration hydrogen sulfide gas, which is heavier than air, tends to accumulate in depressions or hollows, potentially leading to fatal poisoning incidents.<ref group="注">Between 1935 and 1985, nine deaths occurred at {{ill|Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|ja|地獄谷 (立山町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|qq=}} .</ref> Snow-covered onsen in winter can also trap gas, increasing the risk of poisoning.<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html Hydrogen Sulfide Poisoning Incidents]</ref> {{Seealso|Hydrogen sulfide#Toxicity}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Sessho-seki|Sessho-seki]]
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{reflist|group="注"}}
=== References === {{reflist}} *

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:硫黄泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11587679]] Wikidata name: Sulfur Spring [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This is questionable as I think it requires [[WP:MEDRS]] and I am surprised that jawiki has these claims not only not medically sourced but without medically reliable sources[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:10, 14 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|An example of a sulfur spring: the "Tenkei no Yu" at [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]]'''Sulfur Spring''' (硫黄泉, Iōsen) is a classification of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]<nowiki/>based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} labeling system. It is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medical spring|1=onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q655311}} due to its unique components. == Overview == Sulfur springs are characterized by a rotten egg smell caused by {{ill|hydrogen sulfide|en|Hydrogen sulfide|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hydrogen sulfide|qq=}}<nowiki/>and the frequent occurrence of cloudy water due to {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} (mineral deposits). While the labeling system categorizes all sulfur springs under a single term, they are generally divided into two main types based on the presence or absence of hydrogen sulfide: sulfur-based springs and '''hydrogen sulfide springs.''' Identification of the type can be made using the new or old water quality labels.
The pH range of sulfur springs varies widely. Springs with higher acidity (lower pH) are typically hydrogen sulfide springs, while those with higher alkalinity are often sulfur-based. Notable high-sulfur concentration onsen in Japan include [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]](Fukushima), {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}} (Gunma), and {{ill|Tsukioka Onsen|ja|月岡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukioka Onsen|qq=}} (Niigata). == Definition == Sulfur springs are defined as containing at least 2 mg of total sulfur (S) per kilogram of water, including HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, and H<sub>2</sub>S.<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ Definition and Protection of Onsen - Ministry of the Environment]</ref>
== Comparison of Old and New Labels == {| class="wikitable" |+ Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! Old Label !! New Label !! Abbreviated Label |- | Sulfur Spring || Sulfur Spring || S Spring |- | Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple S Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub> Spring |- | Hydrogen Sulfide Spring || Sulfur Spring (Hydrogen Sulfide Type) || S Spring (H<sub>2</sub>S Type) |}
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
Benefits associated with sulfur springs include the following:
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General indications:''' Chronic {{ill|skin diseases|en|Skin disease|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin diseases|qq=}}, chronic {{ill|gynecological disorders|en|Gynecological disorder|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gynecological disorders|qq=}} , {{ill|cuts|en|Cut (wound)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cuts|qq=}} , and {{ill|diabetes|en|Diabetes mellitus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diabetes|qq=}} .
* '''Hydrogen sulfide type:''' Effective for {{ill|hypertension|en|Hypertension|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hypertension|qq=}}<nowiki/>and {{ill|arteriosclerosis|en|Arteriosclerosis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/arteriosclerosis|qq=}}
==== Drinking ==== * Beneficial for diabetes, {{ill|gout|en|Gout|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gout|qq=}}, {{ill|constipation|en|Constipation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/constipation|qq=}} , and {{ill|diarrhea|en|Diarrhea|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diarrhea|qq=}} . === Contraindications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General contraindications:''' Overly sensitive {{ill|skin|en|Human skin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin|qq=}}<nowiki/>or {{ill|mucous membranes|en|Mucous membrane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mucous membranes|qq=}}, particularly in individuals with {{ill|photosensitivity|en|Photosensitivity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/photosensitivity|qq=}} .<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/pdf/pamph_full_en.pdf Onsen_ABC]</ref> == Representative Onsen == * {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|ja|川湯温泉 (北海道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|qq=}}
* {{ill|Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja|箱根湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱根温泉|1=Hakone Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908993}}(Kanagawa Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|ja|日光湯元温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|qq=}}(Tochigi Prefecture)
* [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]](Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}}(Nagano Prefecture)
* Ebino Kōgen Onsen (Miyazaki Prefecture)
== Accidents == Hydrogen sulfide springs can release hydrogen sulfide gas into the air, which may cause {{ill|poisoning|en|Poisoning|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/poisoning|qq=}}<nowiki/>when inhaled in large concentrations or for extended periods. Proper {{ill|ventilation|en|Ventilation (architecture)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ventilation|qq=}} is essential in indoor baths. In outdoor springs or {{ill|wilderness hot springs|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wilderness hot springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, high-concentration hydrogen sulfide gas, which is heavier than air, tends to accumulate in depressions or hollows, potentially leading to fatal poisoning incidents.<ref group="注">Between 1935 and 1985, nine deaths occurred at {{ill|Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|ja|地獄谷 (立山町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|qq=}} .</ref> Snow-covered onsen in winter can also trap gas, increasing the risk of poisoning.<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html Hydrogen Sulfide Poisoning Incidents]</ref> {{Seealso|Hydrogen sulfide#Toxicity}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Sessho-seki|Sessho-seki]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:硫黄泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11587679]] Wikidata name: Sulfur Spring [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This is questionable as I think it requires [[WP:MEDRS]] and I am surprised that jawiki has these claims not only not medically sourced but without medically reliable sources[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:10, 14 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|An example of a sulfur spring: the "Tenkei no Yu" at [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]]'''Sulfur Spring''' (硫黄泉, Iōsen) is a classification of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]<nowiki/>based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} labeling system. It is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medical spring|1=onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q655311}} due to its unique components. == Overview == Sulfur springs are characterized by a rotten egg smell caused by {{ill|hydrogen sulfide|en|Hydrogen sulfide|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hydrogen sulfide|qq=}}<nowiki/>and the frequent occurrence of cloudy water due to {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} (mineral deposits). While the labeling system categorizes all sulfur springs under a single term, they are generally divided into two main types based on the presence or absence of hydrogen sulfide: sulfur-based springs and '''hydrogen sulfide springs.''' Identification of the type can be made using the new or old water quality labels.
The pH range of sulfur springs varies widely. Springs with higher acidity (lower pH) are typically hydrogen sulfide springs, while those with higher alkalinity are often sulfur-based. Notable high-sulfur concentration onsen in Japan include [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]](Fukushima), {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}} (Gunma), and {{ill|Tsukioka Onsen|ja|月岡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukioka Onsen|qq=}} (Niigata). == Definition == Sulfur springs are defined as containing at least 2 mg of total sulfur (S) per kilogram of water, including HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, and H<sub>2</sub>S.<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ Definition and Protection of Onsen - Ministry of the Environment]</ref>
== Comparison of Old and New Labels == {| class="wikitable" |+ Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! Old Label !! New Label !! Abbreviated Label |- | Sulfur Spring || Sulfur Spring || S Spring |- | Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple S Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub> Spring |- | Hydrogen Sulfide Spring || Sulfur Spring (Hydrogen Sulfide Type) || S Spring (H<sub>2</sub>S Type) |}
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
Benefits associated with sulfur springs include the following:
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General indications:''' Chronic {{ill|skin diseases|en|Skin disease|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin diseases|qq=}}, chronic {{ill|gynecological disorders|en|Gynecological disorder|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gynecological disorders|qq=}} , {{ill|cuts|en|Cut (wound)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cuts|qq=}} , and {{ill|diabetes|en|Diabetes mellitus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diabetes|qq=}} .
* '''Hydrogen sulfide type:''' Effective for {{ill|hypertension|en|Hypertension|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hypertension|qq=}}<nowiki/>and {{ill|arteriosclerosis|en|Arteriosclerosis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/arteriosclerosis|qq=}}
==== Drinking ==== * Beneficial for diabetes, {{ill|gout|en|Gout|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gout|qq=}}, {{ill|constipation|en|Constipation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/constipation|qq=}} , and {{ill|diarrhea|en|Diarrhea|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diarrhea|qq=}} . === Contraindications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General contraindications:''' Overly sensitive {{ill|skin|en|Human skin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin|qq=}}<nowiki/>or {{ill|mucous membranes|en|Mucous membrane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mucous membranes|qq=}}, particularly in individuals with {{ill|photosensitivity|en|Photosensitivity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/photosensitivity|qq=}} .<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/pdf/pamph_full_en.pdf Onsen_ABC]</ref> == Representative Onsen == * {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|ja|川湯温泉 (北海道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|qq=}}
* {{ill|Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja|箱根湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱根温泉|1=Hakone Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908993}}(Kanagawa Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|ja|日光湯元温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|qq=}}(Tochigi Prefecture)
* [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]](Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}}(Nagano Prefecture)
* Ebino Kōgen Onsen (Miyazaki Prefecture)
== Accidents == Hydrogen sulfide springs can release hydrogen sulfide gas into the air, which may cause {{ill|poisoning|en|Poisoning|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/poisoning|qq=}}<nowiki/>when inhaled in large concentrations or for extended periods. Proper {{ill|ventilation|en|Ventilation (architecture)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ventilation|qq=}} is essential in indoor baths. In outdoor springs or {{ill|wilderness hot springs|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wilderness hot springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, high-concentration hydrogen sulfide gas, which is heavier than air, tends to accumulate in depressions or hollows, potentially leading to fatal poisoning incidents.<ref group="注">Between 1935 and 1985, nine deaths occurred at {{ill|Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|ja|地獄谷 (立山町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|qq=}} .</ref> Snow-covered onsen in winter can also trap gas, increasing the risk of poisoning.<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html Hydrogen Sulfide Poisoning Incidents]</ref> {{Seealso|Hydrogen sulfide#Toxicity}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Sessho-seki|Sessho-seki]]
== Notes ==

=== References === {{reflist}} *

[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|An example of a sulfur spring: the "Tenkei no Yu" at [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]]'''Sulfur Spring''' (硫黄泉, Iōsen) is a classification of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]<nowiki/>based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} labeling system. It is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medical spring|1=onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q655311}} due to its unique components. == Overview == Sulfur springs are characterized by a rotten egg smell caused by {{ill|hydrogen sulfide|en|Hydrogen sulfide|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hydrogen sulfide|qq=}}<nowiki/>and the frequent occurrence of cloudy water due to {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} (mineral deposits). While the labeling system categorizes all sulfur springs under a single term, they are generally divided into two main types based on the presence or absence of hydrogen sulfide: sulfur-based springs and '''hydrogen sulfide springs.''' Identification of the type can be made using the new or old water quality labels.
The pH range of sulfur springs varies widely. Springs with higher acidity (lower pH) are typically hydrogen sulfide springs, while those with higher alkalinity are often sulfur-based. Notable high-sulfur concentration onsen in Japan include [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]](Fukushima), {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}} (Gunma), and {{ill|Tsukioka Onsen|ja|月岡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukioka Onsen|qq=}} (Niigata). == Definition == Sulfur springs are defined as containing at least 2 mg of total sulfur (S) per kilogram of water, including HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, and H<sub>2</sub>S.<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ Definition and Protection of Onsen - Ministry of the Environment]</ref>
== Comparison of Old and New Labels == {| class="wikitable" |+ Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! Old Label !! New Label !! Abbreviated Label |- | Sulfur Spring || Sulfur Spring || S Spring |- | Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple S Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub> Spring |- | Hydrogen Sulfide Spring || Sulfur Spring (Hydrogen Sulfide Type) || S Spring (H<sub>2</sub>S Type) |}
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
Benefits associated with sulfur springs include the following:
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General indications:''' Chronic {{ill|skin diseases|en|Skin disease|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin diseases|qq=}}, chronic {{ill|gynecological disorders|en|Gynecological disorder|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gynecological disorders|qq=}} , {{ill|cuts|en|Cut (wound)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cuts|qq=}} , and {{ill|diabetes|en|Diabetes mellitus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diabetes|qq=}} .
* '''Hydrogen sulfide type:''' Effective for {{ill|hypertension|en|Hypertension|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hypertension|qq=}}<nowiki/>and {{ill|arteriosclerosis|en|Arteriosclerosis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/arteriosclerosis|qq=}} ==== Drinking ==== * Beneficial for diabetes, {{ill|gout|en|Gout|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gout|qq=}}, {{ill|constipation|en|Constipation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/constipation|qq=}} , and {{ill|diarrhea|en|Diarrhea|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diarrhea|qq=}} . === Contraindications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General contraindications:''' Overly sensitive {{ill|skin|en|Human skin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin|qq=}}<nowiki/>or {{ill|mucous membranes|en|Mucous membrane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mucous membranes|qq=}}, particularly in individuals with {{ill|photosensitivity|en|Photosensitivity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/photosensitivity|qq=}} .<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/pdf/pamph_full_en.pdf Onsen_ABC]</ref> == Representative Onsen == * {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|ja|川湯温泉 (北海道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|qq=}}
* {{ill|Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja|箱根湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱根温泉|1=Hakone Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908993}}(Kanagawa Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|ja|日光湯元温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|qq=}}(Tochigi Prefecture)
* [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]](Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}}(Nagano Prefecture)
* Ebino Kōgen Onsen (Miyazaki Prefecture)
== Accidents == Hydrogen sulfide springs can release hydrogen sulfide gas into the air, which may cause {{ill|poisoning|en|Poisoning|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/poisoning|qq=}}<nowiki/>when inhaled in large concentrations or for extended periods. Proper {{ill|ventilation|en|Ventilation (architecture)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ventilation|qq=}} is essential in indoor baths. In outdoor springs or {{ill|wilderness hot springs|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wilderness hot springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, high-concentration hydrogen sulfide gas, which is heavier than air, tends to accumulate in depressions or hollows, potentially leading to fatal poisoning incidents.<ref group="注">Between 1935 and 1985, nine deaths occurred at {{ill|Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|ja|地獄谷 (立山町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|qq=}} .</ref> Snow-covered onsen in winter can also trap gas, increasing the risk of poisoning.<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html Hydrogen Sulfide Poisoning Incidents]</ref> {{Seealso|Hydrogen sulfide#Toxicity}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Sessho-seki|Sessho-seki]]
== Notes ==

=== References === {{reflist}} *

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:硫黄泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:硫黄泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:硫黄泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11587679]] Wikidata name: Sulfur Spring [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This is questionable as I think it requires [[WP:MEDRS]] and I am surprised that jawiki has these claims not only not medically sourced but without medically reliable sources[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:10, 14 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|An example of a sulfur spring: the "Tenkei no Yu" at [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]]'''Sulfur Spring''' (硫黄泉, Iōsen) is a classification of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]]<nowiki/>based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} labeling system. It is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medical spring|1=onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q655311}} due to its unique components. == Overview == Sulfur springs are characterized by a rotten egg smell caused by {{ill|hydrogen sulfide|en|Hydrogen sulfide|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hydrogen sulfide|qq=}}<nowiki/>and the frequent occurrence of cloudy water due to {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} (mineral deposits). While the labeling system categorizes all sulfur springs under a single term, they are generally divided into two main types based on the presence or absence of hydrogen sulfide: sulfur-based springs and '''hydrogen sulfide springs.''' Identification of the type can be made using the new or old water quality labels.
The pH range of sulfur springs varies widely. Springs with higher acidity (lower pH) are typically hydrogen sulfide springs, while those with higher alkalinity are often sulfur-based. Notable high-sulfur concentration onsen in Japan include [[:en:Takayu Onsen|Takayu Onsen]](Fukushima), {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}} (Gunma), and {{ill|Tsukioka Onsen|ja|月岡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukioka Onsen|qq=}} (Niigata). == Definition == Sulfur springs are defined as containing at least 2 mg of total sulfur (S) per kilogram of water, including HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, and H<sub>2</sub>S.<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ Definition and Protection of Onsen - Ministry of the Environment]</ref>
== Comparison of Old and New Labels == {| class="wikitable" |+ Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! Old Label !! New Label !! Abbreviated Label |- | Sulfur Spring || Sulfur Spring || S Spring |- | Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple S Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub> Spring |- | Hydrogen Sulfide Spring || Sulfur Spring (Hydrogen Sulfide Type) || S Spring (H<sub>2</sub>S Type) |}
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
Benefits associated with sulfur springs include the following:
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General indications:''' Chronic {{ill|skin diseases|en|Skin disease|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin diseases|qq=}}, chronic {{ill|gynecological disorders|en|Gynecological disorder|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gynecological disorders|qq=}} , {{ill|cuts|en|Cut (wound)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cuts|qq=}} , and {{ill|diabetes|en|Diabetes mellitus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diabetes|qq=}} .
* '''Hydrogen sulfide type:''' Effective for {{ill|hypertension|en|Hypertension|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hypertension|qq=}}<nowiki/>and {{ill|arteriosclerosis|en|Arteriosclerosis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/arteriosclerosis|qq=}} ==== Drinking ==== * Beneficial for diabetes, {{ill|gout|en|Gout|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gout|qq=}}, {{ill|constipation|en|Constipation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/constipation|qq=}} , and {{ill|diarrhea|en|Diarrhea|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diarrhea|qq=}} . === Contraindications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General contraindications:''' Overly sensitive {{ill|skin|en|Human skin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin|qq=}}<nowiki/>or {{ill|mucous membranes|en|Mucous membrane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mucous membranes|qq=}}, particularly in individuals with {{ill|photosensitivity|en|Photosensitivity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/photosensitivity|qq=}} .<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/pdf/pamph_full_en.pdf Onsen_ABC]</ref> == Representative Onsen == * {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|ja|川湯温泉 (北海道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|qq=}}
* {{ill|Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja|箱根湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱根温泉|1=Hakone Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908993}}(Kanagawa Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|ja|日光湯元温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|qq=}}(Tochigi Prefecture)
* [[:en:Kusatsu Onsen|Kusatsu Onsen]](Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}}(Nagano Prefecture)
* Ebino Kōgen Onsen (Miyazaki Prefecture)
== Accidents == Hydrogen sulfide springs can release hydrogen sulfide gas into the air, which may cause {{ill|poisoning|en|Poisoning|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/poisoning|qq=}}<nowiki/>when inhaled in large concentrations or for extended periods. Proper {{ill|ventilation|en|Ventilation (architecture)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ventilation|qq=}} is essential in indoor baths. In outdoor springs or {{ill|wilderness hot springs|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wilderness hot springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, high-concentration hydrogen sulfide gas, which is heavier than air, tends to accumulate in depressions or hollows, potentially leading to fatal poisoning incidents.<ref group="注">Between 1935 and 1985, nine deaths occurred at {{ill|Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|ja|地獄谷 (立山町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|qq=}} .</ref> Snow-covered onsen in winter can also trap gas, increasing the risk of poisoning.<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html Hydrogen Sulfide Poisoning Incidents]</ref> {{Seealso|Hydrogen sulfide#Toxicity}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Sessho-seki|Sessho-seki]]
== Notes ==

=== References === {{reflist}} *

Old Label !! New Label !! Abbreviated Label |- | Sulfur Spring || Sulfur Spring || S Spring |- | Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple S Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub> Spring |- | Hydrogen Sulfide Spring || Sulfur Spring (Hydrogen Sulfide Type) || S Spring (H<sub>2</sub>S Type) |}

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:硫黄泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:硫黄泉]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:硫黄泉]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11587679]] Wikidata name: Sulfur Spring [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This is questionable as I think it requires [[WP:MEDRS]] and I am surprised that jawiki has these claims not only not medically sourced but without medically reliable sources[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:10, 14 March 2025 (UTC)}}
[[File:Tamagoyu, 天渓の湯 01.jpg|thumb|right|An example of a sulfur spring: the "Tenkei no Yu" at {{ill|Takayu Onsen|en|Takayu Onsen|WD=Q7677489|lt=Takayu Onsen}}'''Sulfur Spring''' (硫黄泉, Iōsen) is a classification of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}}<nowiki/>based on the {{ill|Hot Spring Types|ja|温泉の泉質|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hot Spring Types|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} labeling system. It is categorized as a {{ill|medical spring|ja|温泉#療養泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medical spring|1=onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q655311}} due to its unique components. == Overview == Sulfur springs are characterized by a rotten egg smell caused by {{ill|hydrogen sulfide|en|Hydrogen sulfide|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hydrogen sulfide|qq=}}<nowiki/>and the frequent occurrence of cloudy water due to {{ill|Sinter|ja|湯の花|lt=Yunohana|1=Sinter|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|13=User:Immanuelle/Sinter|qq=}} (mineral deposits). While the labeling system categorizes all sulfur springs under a single term, they are generally divided into two main types based on the presence or absence of hydrogen sulfide: sulfur-based springs and '''hydrogen sulfide springs.''' Identification of the type can be made using the new or old water quality labels.
The pH range of sulfur springs varies widely. Springs with higher acidity (lower pH) are typically hydrogen sulfide springs, while those with higher alkalinity are often sulfur-based. Notable high-sulfur concentration onsen in Japan include {{ill|Takayu Onsen|en|Takayu Onsen|WD=Q7677489|lt=Takayu Onsen}}(Fukushima), {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}} (Gunma), and {{ill|Tsukioka Onsen|ja|月岡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukioka Onsen|qq=}} (Niigata). == Definition == Sulfur springs are defined as containing at least 2 mg of total sulfur (S) per kilogram of water, including HS-, S<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>--, and H<sub>2</sub>S.<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/point/ Definition and Protection of Onsen - Ministry of the Environment]</ref>
== Comparison of Old and New Labels == {| class="wikitable" |+ Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels<ref>{{Cite web|title=Reference Material: Comparison of Old and New Onsen Water Quality Labels |url=https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/docs/senshitsumei26.pdf |format=PDF |publisher=Natural Environment Bureau, Ministry of the Environment |accessdate=2018-03-26 }} </ref> ! Old Label !! New Label !! Abbreviated Label |- | Sulfur Spring || Sulfur Spring || S Spring |- | Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple Sulfur Spring || Simple S Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl Spring |- | Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || Sulfur and Sodium Bicarbonate-Chloride Spring || S-Na-Cl･HCO<sub>3</sub> Spring |- | Hydrogen Sulfide Spring || Sulfur Spring (Hydrogen Sulfide Type) || S Spring (H<sub>2</sub>S Type) |}
== Health Benefits == ''Note: [[Health effects of hot springs|Effects]] are not guaranteed for all individuals.''
Benefits associated with sulfur springs include the following:
=== Indications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General indications:''' Chronic {{ill|skin diseases|en|Skin disease|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin diseases|qq=}}, chronic {{ill|gynecological disorders|en|Gynecological disorder|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gynecological disorders|qq=}} , {{ill|cuts|en|Cut (wound)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cuts|qq=}} , and {{ill|diabetes|en|Diabetes mellitus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diabetes|qq=}} .
* '''Hydrogen sulfide type:''' Effective for {{ill|hypertension|en|Hypertension|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hypertension|qq=}}<nowiki/>and {{ill|arteriosclerosis|en|Arteriosclerosis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/arteriosclerosis|qq=}} ==== Drinking ==== * Beneficial for diabetes, {{ill|gout|en|Gout|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gout|qq=}}, {{ill|constipation|en|Constipation|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/constipation|qq=}} , and {{ill|diarrhea|en|Diarrhea|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diarrhea|qq=}} . === Contraindications === ==== Bathing ==== * '''General contraindications:''' Overly sensitive {{ill|skin|en|Human skin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/skin|qq=}}<nowiki/>or {{ill|mucous membranes|en|Mucous membrane|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mucous membranes|qq=}}, particularly in individuals with {{ill|photosensitivity|en|Photosensitivity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/photosensitivity|qq=}} .<ref>[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/pdf/pamph_full_en.pdf Onsen_ABC]</ref> == Representative Onsen == * {{ill|Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|ja|川湯温泉 (北海道)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawayu Onsen (Hokkaido)|qq=}}
* {{ill|Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja|箱根湯本温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakone Yumoto Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱根温泉|1=Hakone Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q908993}}(Kanagawa Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|ja|日光湯元温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Yumoto Onsen|qq=}}(Tochigi Prefecture)
* {{ill|Kusatsu Onsen|en|Kusatsu Onsen|WD=Q3137477|lt=Kusatsu Onsen}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Manza Onsen|ja|万座温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manza Onsen|qq=}}(Gunma Prefecture)
* {{ill|Nozawa Onsen|ja|野沢温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nozawa Onsen|qq=}}(Nagano Prefecture)
* Ebino Kōgen Onsen (Miyazaki Prefecture)
== Accidents == Hydrogen sulfide springs can release hydrogen sulfide gas into the air, which may cause {{ill|poisoning|en|Poisoning|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/poisoning|qq=}}<nowiki/>when inhaled in large concentrations or for extended periods. Proper {{ill|ventilation|en|Ventilation (architecture)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ventilation|qq=}} is essential in indoor baths. In outdoor springs or {{ill|wilderness hot springs|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wilderness hot springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, high-concentration hydrogen sulfide gas, which is heavier than air, tends to accumulate in depressions or hollows, potentially leading to fatal poisoning incidents.<ref group="注">Between 1935 and 1985, nine deaths occurred at {{ill|Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|ja|地獄谷 (立山町)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jigokudani Onsen (Tateyama)|qq=}} .</ref> Snow-covered onsen in winter can also trap gas, increasing the risk of poisoning.<ref>[http://www.iamp.tohoku.ac.jp/~liquid/MURA/hitorigoto/gas-kiji/h2s.html Hydrogen Sulfide Poisoning Incidents]</ref> {{Seealso|Hydrogen sulfide#Toxicity}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Sessho-seki|en|Sessho-seki|WD=Q2616914|lt=Sessho-seki}}
== Notes ==
=== Annotations === {{reflist|group="注"}}
=== References === {{reflist}} *
{{Onsen}}
{{translated page|ja|硫黄泉|version=101483294|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[en:Sulfur spring]] [[ja:硫黄泉]]
